summary: Leading Academic skeptic of the 'New Academy' who emphasized probabilism in skepticism. Known for giving contradictory lectures on justice while visiting Rome and for the 'Plank of Carneades' thought experiment about self-defense killings. Rival of the Stoic Chrysippus.

works.name: Probabilism and Academic Skepticism
works.description: Carneades was the leading Academic skeptic scholarch of the 'New Academy' who argued that although there are no cognitive impressions, knowledge is still possible through probabilistic reasoning. He was Chrysippus's main rival, and apocryphally responded to the phrase 'without Chrysippus, there would be no Stoa' with 'And without Chrysippus, there would be no me.' Scholars disagree whether he or Arcesilaus first promulgated akatalepsia.

clues.clue: Argued knowledge is still possible through probabilistic reasoning despite no cognitive impressions
examples: this skeptic scholarch of the Academy argued that although there are no cognitive impressions, knowledge is still possible through probabilistic reasoning

clues.clue: Rival of Chrysippus; apocryphally said 'without Chrysippus, there would be no me'
examples: Apocryphally, upon hearing the phrase 'without Chrysippus, there would be no Stoa,' a member of this school responded, 'And without Chrysippus, there would be no me'

clues.clue: Scholars disagree whether Arcesilaus or Carneades first promulgated akatalepsia
examples: Scholars disagree whether Arcesilaus or this leading Academic skeptic of the 'New Academy' first promulgated akatalepsia

works.name: Plank of Carneades and Rome Visit
works.description: Carneades names a 'plank' in a famous thought experiment used to discuss self-defense killings: if two shipwrecked sailors find a plank that can only support one, is the survivor justified in pushing the other off? He also gave contradictory lectures on justice while visiting Rome, arguing for justice one day and against it the next.

clues.clue: Names a 'plank' thought experiment used to discuss self-defense killings
examples: This rival of Chrysippus names a 'plank' in a thought experiment used to discuss self-defense killings

clues.clue: Gave contradictory lectures on justice while visiting Rome
examples: this thinker who emphasized probabilism in skepticism gave contradictory lectures on justice while visiting Rome

comprehensive_summary:
Carneades was the leading Academic skeptic of the 'New Academy,' serving as scholarch of Plato's Academy. His central philosophical contribution was the introduction of probabilistic reasoning into skepticism: he argued that although there are no cognitive impressions, knowledge is still possible through probabilistic assessment.

Carneades was the main intellectual rival of the Stoic philosopher Chrysippus. Apocryphally, upon hearing the phrase 'without Chrysippus, there would be no Stoa,' a member of the skeptical school responded 'And without Chrysippus, there would be no me.' Scholars disagree whether Carneades or his predecessor Arcesilaus first promulgated akatalepsia, the view that nothing can be known.

Carneades is also known for two distinctive episodes. He gave contradictory lectures on justice while visiting Rome, arguing for justice one day and against it the next. His name is attached to the 'Plank of Carneades,' a thought experiment about self-defense killings in which two shipwrecked sailors find a plank that can only support one person. The Academic skeptical tradition he led eventually ended during the First Mithridatic War when Philo of Larissa fled to Rome.
